introduction: Barnaby the Bunny propose we should paint the entire barn bright blue because 'blue is the happiest color.

logicalFallacies.explanation: Attacking the person instead of the argument.
Presenting only two options when more alternatives exist.

sentences.text: Friends of the farm, look up at the wide blue sky — that very shade is the color of joy itself.
If we paint our barn that same beautiful blue, every animal will wake up feeling like they are floating among the clouds.

options.reason: Focuses on personal taste in colors instead of addressing the farm's real priority: a roof that actually keeps the rain out.

sentences.text: I think the natural wood color of the barn already looks classic and warm.
We should spend more money to repair the holes on the roof, safety is more important than painting the barn.

options.reason: Keeps the argument focused on immediate safety, which is stronger than cosmetic improvements.

sentences.text: Before changing color, we should fix the roof first so every animal stays dry and safe.

options.reason: States a practical concern but does not strongly explain why the roof should be the first priority.

sentences.text: Let us postpone painting until next season and use this budget for repairs.

options.reason: Trivial logistical questions that let Barnaby duck the real issue: cost versus the roof.

sentences.text: How much of the money should we spend on the paint and brushes?
Almost all of the money will go to the paint and brushes.

Pip, why should anyone listen to a tiny mouse with such dull, gray fur and no sense of style at all? Isn't it obvious you only hate the blue plan because you yourself are too drab to appreciate true beauty?

logicalFallacies.explanation: Barnaby attacks Pip's appearance and personal taste instead of engaging with the argument about the leaking roof and the budget.

options.reason: Defensive about appearance — exactly where Barnaby wants the conversation. Misses the chance to name the fallacy and pivot back to the roof.

sentences.text: I am not drab! I groom my fur every single morning down by the pond.

options.reason: Names the Ad Hominem out loud, refuses the personal bait, and drags the debate back to the roof and the budget — the questions Barnaby was trying to dodge.

sentences.text: locked

unlockedSentences.text: Barnaby, the color of my fur has nothing to do with the holes in our roof.

sentences.text: Pip's so-called 'budget' is nothing but a smokescreen for a much darker truth about this farm.
Either we paint this barn blue today, or we all agree to live in a miserable, colorless world forever.

logicalFallacies.explanation: Barnaby pretends there are only two options — his blue plan or eternal misery — when in reality the barn could simply stay its natural color and still be a happy place.

sentences.text: There is no middle ground when the joy of the entire community is on the line.

options.reason: Mirrors Barnaby's mistake by inventing a False Dilemma of Pip's own. Doesn't expose the bad logic — just adds more bad logic on top.

sentences.text: Either we ignore Barnaby completely, or this whole farm collapses by the first frost!

logicalFallacies.explanation: Pip frames the choice as ignore-Barnaby-or-collapse, ignoring sensible middle paths.

sentences.text: There are only two paths here: a fixed roof, or every animal frozen by December!

logicalFallacies.explanation: Reduces a complex farm budget to two extreme outcomes.

sentences.text: Pick survival or pick paint — there is absolutely no other way!

logicalFallacies.explanation: Repeats the either-or framing instead of breaking out of it.

options.reason: Tries to soften Barnaby's premise but accepts the framing that life without blue is sad. Doesn't name or break the fallacy.

sentences.text: I am not a smokescreen, I am just a mouse trying to be helpful.
The world isn't really colorless if we just look at the green fields and the yellow hay.
Maybe we can be a little bit happy without any paint at all, if we try.

options.reason: Names the False Dilemma directly, opens up a third path (a dry, unpainted barn), and grounds the audience in the practical comparison: a leaky blue barn versus a dry plain one.

sentences.text: locked

unlockedSentences.text: Barnaby is offering a False Dilemma — paint blue or live in misery, as if those are the only two choices.
We can keep the barn the color it already is and still be happy and still have a roof that doesn't leak.
A dry wooden barn beats a blue barn full of puddles every single day of the year.

sentences.text: Pip, if you don't let us paint the barn blue, aren't you simply choosing to keep us all sad and gray forever?

logicalFallacies.explanation: Barnaby is again pretending the only two outcomes are 'paint blue' or 'eternal sadness', leaving out every reasonable alternative.

options.reason: Answers Barnaby's False Dilemma with another False Dilemma. The audience hears two animals trapped in either-or thinking instead of one of them breaking out of it.

sentences.text: Either Barnaby admits he is wrong, or none of us should ever speak to him again!

logicalFallacies.explanation: Reduces a disagreement to silence-or-surrender with no middle ground.

sentences.text: The choice is happiness or honesty — pick one, Barnaby!

logicalFallacies.explanation: Falsely treats happiness and honesty as opposites.

sentences.text: Either you fix the roof yourself, or you stop talking nonsense forever!

options.reason: Wanders away from the question. Doesn't refute the trap or offer a real third option.

sentences.text: Sadness comes from many places, not just from the color of a wall.
Some animals are sad even on bright sunny days for reasons I don't fully understand.
Is there a mouse counselor on this farm we could maybe ask about feelings?

options.reason: Refuses Barnaby's binary, offers a clear third path (a dry, repaired barn = a different kind of happiness), and even leaves room to talk about color later — fair, calm, and persuasive.

sentences.text: No, because a dry, repaired barn makes everyone feel safe — and safety is its own kind of happiness.
Saving for the roof doesn't choose sadness; it chooses sensible care for the whole farm.
Once the roof is fixed, we can absolutely talk about cheerful decorations next spring.

options.reason: Personal attack on Barnaby's character and species. Even if it lands a laugh, it abandons the substance of the debate.

sentences.text: Barnaby, are you a real leader, or just a fluffy daydreamer with no practical sense at all?

logicalFallacies.explanation: Goes after Barnaby's character ('fluffy daydreamer') rather than the merits of his plan.

sentences.text: Have you ever fixed anything on this farm with your own paws, or do you just talk?
Or is your entire plan based on staring at the sky for too long?

options.reason: Logistical small-talk. Doesn't expose the flaw in painting first and fixing later.

sentences.text: How many cans of paint do you think we'll actually need for the whole barn?
Will the brushes come from Mr. Hedgehog's shop or from Old Mrs. Vole's?
Should we use rollers, or just brushes for the high parts up under the eaves?

options.reason: Brutally practical: paint on damp, rotting wood is wasted paint. Forces Barnaby to either concede the roof must come first or look reckless.

sentences.text: If we paint over damp, rotting wood, won't the paint just bubble and peel within a single month?
Why coat a building that needs structural repair before any decoration even makes sense?
Wouldn't fixing the roof first actually protect any future paint job we eventually do?
I am a VISIONARY, Pip, which is far above 'practical'! My paws are perfect for holding inspirational color swatches.
We will need ALL the paint! Every can on the farm! The hedgehog will be absolutely delighted.
Either we paint NOW while the spirit moves us, or this beautiful moment of inspiration passes forever! Why hesitate?

logicalFallacies.explanation: Barnaby presents a fake either-or: paint right now or lose the chance forever, ignoring the option of painting later after the roof is fixed.

sentences.text: Animals of the farm, the moment is here. Look at our barn and imagine the entire sky descending upon it in glorious blue.
You must choose now: a barn of joy and color, or a lifetime of dull, dripping despair.

logicalFallacies.explanation: Barnaby once again presents only two extremes — a blue paradise or eternal despair — leaving out every reasonable middle ground.

sentences.text: There are no other paths, my friends. Vote for the blue dream, or vote for gray misery.

logicalFallacies.explanation: The 'no other paths' framing is the heart of the False Dilemma — it pretends compromise and alternatives simply don't exist.

options.reason: Closes with a False Dilemma of Pip's own. After spending the whole debate exposing Barnaby's bad logic, this throws away that progress at the most important moment.

sentences.text: In conclusion, we either ban paint forever, or this farm is doomed!

logicalFallacies.explanation: Reduces a complex decision to ban-paint-or-doom, ignoring the obvious option of fixing the roof first and painting later.

sentences.text: Choose between Barnaby's nonsense or my sensible roof — there is no third way!

logicalFallacies.explanation: Mirrors Barnaby's bad framing instead of breaking it.

sentences.text: Vote for the roof, or vote for ruin!

logicalFallacies.explanation: Another either-or with no middle ground.

options.reason: Wishy-washy and forgettable. The audience needs a clear ask before they vote.

sentences.text: Maybe we should all just go to bed and decide tomorrow when we're well rested.
The moon will probably give us all better advice in the morning.
Or maybe we could ask the owl for help, but the owl is asleep at this hour.

options.reason: The perfect close: names both fallacies the audience just watched in real time, replaces Barnaby's either-or with a real third path, and ties it back to safety as the foundation of any real happiness.

sentences.text: Barnaby has tried to attack my fur and trap us all between two extreme choices.
But we don't have to choose between joy and survival — fixing the roof is what makes joy possible in the first place.
Vote to fix the roof first, because a safe barn is the foundation every cheerful color is painted on.

steps.message:
**Welcome!** This is the beginning of the **[accent]debate's tutorial[/accent]**. 

A debate has **[accent]3 main actors[/accent]**:

 the **[accent]1) moderator[/accent]**, the **[accent]2) proposition side[/accent]**, and the **[accent]3) opposition side[/accent]**.

 In a debate, the **[accent]proposition side[/accent]** is the one who proposes the idea, and the **[accent]opposition side[/accent]** is the one who argues against it. 

**[success]The moderator is the one who decides who wins the debate.[/success]**

For this tutorial, **[accent]you[/accent]** will play as the **[accent]opposition side[/accent]**. 

 In order to win the moderator's favour, you need to bring on the table the most **[accent]convincing arguments[/accent]** that make the proposition idea look less convincing and not worth it.

 To do that, you'll **analyze statements**, **spot logical fallacies**, and choose replies that **[success]win the moderator's favour[/success]**.

 The **[accent]emoji score[/accent]** next to **Debate Log** tracks how the moderator leans toward your side as you play.

A debate is made of **[accent]10 rounds[/accent]**.
 Rounds are of different types: **[accent]introduction; opening constructive; rebuttal; crossfire; closing constructive.[/accent]**

 You will be guided through the process. 

 Press **[accent]Continue[/accent]** and **[accent]Got it[/accent]** to go forward in the tutorial.
 Press **[accent]Back[/accent]** to go one step back in the tutorial (when available).

In this tutorial debate:

 **[warning]Barnaby[/warning]** represents the **[warning]Proposition side[/warning]** and pushes the **[warning]blue-barn plan[/warning]** to make the farm more colorful and happy. 

 On the other hand, **[accent]Pip (you)[/accent]** represents the **[accent]Opposition side[/accent]** and argue to use the farm's savings for **[accent]something more urgent[/accent]** first.
The debate always starts with the **[accent]Moderator's Introduction[/accent]**, which you will read in the **[accent]Wizard Panel[/accent]**.
The Introduction details will also be available in the **[accent]Debate Log Panel[/accent]**, which represents the **[accent]full history of the debate[/accent]**.
Once you have a clear picture of the debate's main topic, start the actual debate by pressing **[accent]Continue[/accent]**.
Introduction and rounds will have their recap just after they finish.

**[accent]Round 1[/accent]** is about **[accent]Proposition side's Constructive Opening[/accent]**.

 Proposition side just shares the idea they want to promote.

For this debate, we are going to have **[warning]Barnaby's opening statement[/warning]**.

 Read it in the **[accent]Wizard Panel[/accent]**.

An Opening Constructive consists of a statement where that side presents their arguments.

 **[accent]Rebuttal[/accent]** rounds and a **[accent]Closing Constructive[/accent]** final round are played in a very similar way.
Round 1 always belongs to the **[accent]Proposition side[/accent]**, so once you play this role in any debate, you will play Round 1.
Remember, you can rely on **[accent]Debate Log Panel[/accent]** to read everything happened so far, ordered by **[accent]round[/accent]**.

Now, let's **[accent]analyze the opening statement[/accent]**. 

 Click the **[accent]Magnifying Glass Icon[/accent]**, which we will call **[accent]Analyze button[/accent]**.

**[accent]Round 2[/accent]** is about **[accent]Opposition side's constructive opening[/accent]**.

 Opposition side just shares their arguments against the proposition.
**[accent]Your turn[/accent]**. You have **[accent]3 options[/accent]** to choose from in the interactive panel.
Click **[accent]Option A[/accent]** to review it.
You can read the full text of the **[accent]selected option[/accent]** in the **[accent]Wizard Panel[/accent]**.
After you **[accent]review each option[/accent]** and choose what's best for the debate, just press **[accent]Continue[/accent]** to confirm your choice.

At the end of your rounds you will get a sneak peek of **[accent]how the moderator was influenced by your statement[/accent]**. 

 Currently, the moderator is **[warning]neutral[/warning]**.

Now let's see a different kind of round: **[accent]crossfire[/accent]**. 

 In a crossfire, there is an **[accent]interaction between the 2 sides[/accent]**. One side asks a question, the other answers.

In this round, you as the **[accent]Opposition side[/accent]** will ask a **[accent]question[/accent]** to the **[warning]Proposition side[/warning]**. 

 Read the possible questions in the **[accent]Interactive Panel[/accent]**.
Let's pick the **[accent]first question[/accent]**.
You can read the full text of the **[accent]selected question[/accent]** in the **[accent]Wizard Panel[/accent]**.
Press **[accent]Continue[/accent]** to ask the selected question.
Round 3 is not finished yet. Now, we can also read the **[warning]answer[/warning]** from the **[accent]Wizard Panel[/accent]**.

At this point, you start to have a few possible actions to do. 
 You can analyze both your own question and the answer from the **[warning]Proposition side[/warning]**.

Indeed, you can see **[accent]2 magnifying glass icons[/accent]** in the **[accent]Debate Log Panel[/accent]**. One for your own question, one for the answer from the **[warning]Proposition side[/warning]**.
Spotting fallacies, or just reading correctly a statement as fallacy-free will give you **[accent]bonuses[/accent]** that will contribute to **[success]win the debate[/success]**.
For the sake of the tutorial, let's just proceed to the next round.

This is the **[accent]Statement Analysis View[/accent]**. 

**[accent]Successfully analyzing a statement[/accent]** will give you **[success]bonuses[/success]** that will contribute to **[success]win the debate[/success]**.

A statement is made of **[accent]one or more sentences[/accent]**. 
 **[accent]Each sentence[/accent]** can have up to **[warning]two fallacies[/warning]**. 
 It's also possible that the **[accent]whole statement does not contain any fallacy at all[/accent]**.

**[accent]Analyzing correctly a statement[/accent]** will give you **[accent]Insights[/accent]**.

 Once you have at least **[success]2 Insights[/success]**, you can **[accent]reveal which sentences contain fallacies[/accent]** on the active statement.

 Insights are gathered during the debate and can be used for future analyses.

By Clicking the **[accent]Help button[/accent]** it will be revealed which sentences contain fallacies, if any. 

 The Help button label will show you how many Insights you currently have and how many are required to reveal the fallacies.

 Currently you have **[accent]0 Insights[/accent]**.
The amount of **[accent]Insights[/accent]** and the **[accent]available Attempts[/accent]** are displayed in the bottom right corner of the Analysis View.

**[accent]Spotting fallacies[/accent]** will also give you a deeper understanding of what's going on within the other side's statements, which also means a way to **[success]weaken the other's side statements[/success]**.

How does that happen? Once it will be your turn to speak you will be able to **[success]use bonus-locked statements[/success]** on top of regular statements, and these will **[success]influence more effectively the moderator's opinion[/success]**.
**[accent]Choosing statements[/accent]** will be clarified **[accent]later[/accent]** in the tutorial.

Let's start! 
In this **[accent]Analysis View[/accent]** you **[warning]first choose a sentence[/warning]** to inspect, then **[warning]pick fallacies[/warning]** from the grid that might apply to it. You can tag **[accent]up to two[/accent]** fallacies on the same sentence (click again to remove one).

When you are satisfied, click **[accent]Spot Fallacies[/accent]** button to submit your guess, or use the **[muted]No Fallacies[/muted]** button if you think the whole statement is fallacy free. 
Those are the two actions that **[success]finalize[/success]** the analysis.
As first thing, select the **[accent]sentence[/accent]** you want to tag with fallacies. (click the highlighted sentence)
Pick **[warning]false dilemma[/warning]** from the fallacies list.
Press **[accent]Spot Fallacies[/accent]** to submit your match (sentence 1 with false dilemma fallacy).

Mmmm ... **[warning]that's not quite right.[/warning]** You still have 2 attempts left.

 Thinking again... Maybe the whole statement is **[accent]fallacy free[/accent]?** 
Try **[accent]No Fallacies[/accent]** this time.
A confirmation is asked. Press **[accent]Confirm[/accent]** to proceed.

**[success]Correct![/success]**
 You have earned **[accent]1 Insight[/accent]** now.

We will come back to this Analysis View later in the tutorial. 
For now, continue the tutorial by **[accent]closing the analysis view[/accent]**.
Press **[accent]Continue[/accent]** to proceed to **[accent]Round 2[/accent]**.
Press **[accent]Continue[/accent]** to proceed.

Round 4 is the **[accent]other side crossfire[/accent]** that asks you a **[warning]question[/warning]**. 
 Check the **[accent]Wizard Panel[/accent]** to read the **[warning]question[/warning]**. 
 Remember, the question is also loggen in the **[accent]Debate Log Panel[/accent]**.

We can choose a **[accent]statement to answer[/accent]** among the 3 options.
 One option looks **[warning]locked[/warning]**!

When you find a **[warning]locked statement[/warning]**, it means you need to **[accent]analyze a prior statement[/accent]** of this debate. 

 Once you **[success]successfully analyze[/success]** the fallacious statement, the locked statement will become **[success]unlockable[/success]**. 

 Click the **[accent]analysis button[/accent]** to spot the fallacy in Barnaby question.
Now inspect **[warning]Barnaby's crossfire question[/warning]** and select the **[accent]highlighted sentence[/accent]**.
Pick **[warning]ad hominem[/warning]**.
Submit your guess.

**[success]Correct![/success]** 
 The question raised by Barnaby hides an **[warning]Ad Hominem fallacy[/warning]**.
 Now that you are aware of that, you will be able to provide a sharper answer! 
 Close the analysis.

Option B is now **[success]unlockable[/success]**. 

 Click to unlock it.
**[accent]Click again Option B[/accent]** to select it and read the full response.
You can now read the full text of the **[success]unlocked option[/success]** in the **[accent]Wizard Panel[/accent]**.
Press **[success]Continue[/success]** to play it.
This round was more effective. We can see **[success]Moderator's favor[/success]** has increased for you!

This is the **[success]end of the tutorial![/success]**

You learnt how to **[accent]choose a statement[/accent]**, how to **[accent]spot a fallacy[/accent]**, and how to **[accent]unlock statements[/accent]**.

Actual debates last longer and reach **[accent]round 10[/accent]**.

 Play a debate and challenge debaters!
